comment.body: Dynamic Weighting and Time Decay: As the day progresses, the declining time to near-term expiration amplifies the 1/T factor in the variance calculation, making the index more sensitive to changes in option prices. But the real “spike” often comes from the abrupt shift toward the next-term options, which embed expectations for overnight and next-day risks (like earnings or economic data). If tomorrow’s implied vol differs significantly from today’s (e.g., higher due to anticipated events), the VIX1D can jump as the weight flips.

If tomorrow’s implied vol differs significantly from today’s (e.g., higher due to anticipated events), the VIX1D can jump as the weight flips.

comment.body: When less than 60 minutes remain to near-term expiration (around 3:00 p.m. ET), the near-term variance is “persisted” (frozen at its last calculated value), and the index increasingly relies on the next-term. By 4:00 p.m. ET, it fully converges to the next-term volatility alone, and from 4:00-4:15 p.m., it’s a single-term calculation.

But the real “spike” often comes from the abrupt shift toward the next-term options, which embed expectations for overnight and next-day risks (like earnings or economic data). user.name: Doc McGraw
user.bio: Providing a Road Map for trading SPX - Iron Condors or scalps. Using a combination of Structural Analysis + years of experience. QUANTUITION Math + 40 years of compressed pattern recognition

comment.body: Overnight Bias: Research highlights a consistent intraday rise in VIX1D during trading hours, followed by an overnight fall. This “overnight bias” stems from the methodology’s use of business time (only counting trading hours) and the inclusion of overnight variance risk premiums in the next-term options. As the weighting shifts end-of-day, it captures more of this premium, often causing an upward spike that looks predictable and repetitive. This pattern overlaps with (and is stronger than) day-of-the-week effects, like lower vol on Mondays.

comment.body: Several practical trading strategies utilize VIX1D signals to inform intraday option trades, especially in the context of zero-day-to-expiration (0DTE) SPX options. The most effective approaches involve either selling premium when VIX1D is high or buying options when VIX1D is low, with some nuanced management based on market behavior observed throughout the day.

comment.body: VIX1D’s pattern of a slow drift higher during the session—often culminating in a late spike—is not typically present in the 30-day VIX or the 9-day VIX9D, both of which interpolate across expirations in a smoother fashion and do not focus on the final moments of short-dated options.

comment.body:
The rolling straddle price is an authentic, tradeable view of expected move and demand for protection. If it’s sticky or rising, it means market participants are bidding up short-term IV—information you can directly use for volatility selling or buying decisions.
•	VIX1D is a constructed index; while it signals the environment for short-dated volatility, it’s subject to formulaic jumps and doesn’t always match the liquidity or tradability of actual option prices.
